+1 for writing our own function to shorten the word. Reason is that
```
>>> textwrap.shorten("hello world", width=10, placeholder="...")
'hello...'
>>> textwrap.shorten("helloworld", width=9, placeholder="...")
'...'
```
So what `textwrap.shorten` does is that, `if len(data) > width` it truncates `data` till the nearest whitespace and appends `...` to it
As you said, it becomes an issue when `data` doesn't have whitespaces.

Personally I don't like tests where the method is re-implemented. It's likely that if you make a mistake with the rotation matrix in the code, you'd make the same mistake re-coding it in the test.

Suggest to just apply the method and assert_allclose on the numbers that come out.

If you can pick a test case where you either know exactly (e.g. 90 deg rotation) or kinda (e.g. 1 deg rotation) what should come out, then put that, but otherwise IMO also just arbitrary output numbers that establish the current behaviour are better than re-implementing the method.

But I know many people disagree and prefer to write tests this way, so no need to argue. Put as you think best and 👍 to merge.

```

def computeSVD(self, numSVDSamples=None, finalize=True):
    """
    Compute the singular value decomposition (SVD). The SVD is a factorization of a real or complex matrix. It factors the matrix `a` as `u * np.diag(s) * v`, where `u` and `v` are unitary and `s` is a 1-d array of `a`'s singular values.

    Reason for computing the SVD: 
    There are cases where you want to feed a lot of vectors to the KNNClassifier. However, this can be slow. You can speed up training by (1) computing the SVD of the input patterns which will give you the eigenvectors, (2) only keeping a fraction of the eigenvectors, and (3) projecting the input patterns onto the remaining eigenvectors. 

    Note that all input patterns are projected onto the eigenvectors in the same fashion. Keeping only the highest eigenvectors increases training performance since it reduces the dimensionality of the input. 

    :param numSVDSamples: (int) the number of samples to use for the SVD computation.
    :param finalize: (bool) whether to apply SVD to the input patterns.
    :return s: (array) The singular values for every matrix, sorted in descending order.
    """
```

Sorry for the block of text below. Form validation is a notoriously tricky problem and can be difficult to do in a declarative, extensible way.

Delegating the responsibility of validation to the textbox by passing in validator functions might give too much responsibility and local state to the textbox component. In particular it leads to imperative-style coding like in the [create-user modal](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/2005/commits/736dd41582b415a7878e2e7a7ce796b99e9418e4#diff-3add6dedbfbaa28f7d2c673b70865ffaR188), where you have to command the textbox to perform a validation with `textboxRef.validate()` and query the validity state with `textboxRef.isValid()`.

Another risk is that this bakes some assumptions about how we do validation in forms - in particular, waiting until de-focus to validate - into the textbox component. That's good a good thing until we come across one exception to the rule where we want to validate on keypress.

On the other hand, one difficulty with moving validation state to the parent form component is that `isValid` for a field does depend on keyboard focus state changes. A way around that might be to not use `v-model` (which updates the parent data on every keypress) and instead listen for [`change` events](https://www.w3.org/WAI/UA/TS/html401/cp0102/0102-ONCHANGE-INPUT.html) (which should only get triggered when the textbox is blurred). This would decouple the "current value visible in the box" from the "value that determines what error message should be displayed".

Quoting from the [forms style guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rrq8fovV5wBtUKv65wfRqXxP4SfnV30anUZHfzCVmN4/edit):

> * Error text should only be shown after the user interacts with the field **and** loses focus on it. 
> * Error text should disappear once the user alters anything within an errored field **and** loses focus on it.
> * If there is a field that is clearly marked with an error on the client side, the submit button for the form should be disabled until the client side errors on each affected field are gone.

So I _think_ as long as we wait to update the parent form `data` until de-focus, all of the validation state can simply be computed properties of those parent `data` values and those would reactively control the field messaging and submission button.

Over time we might see some common patterns (e.g. managing pairs of validation functions and message strings) repeated over and over at the form level. When that happens, we could possibly look at creating a form mixin with some helpers?

Yes, I get that. That's the same with TypeScript which just adds type hints on top of JavaScript. But still `mypy` (or `tsc` in the TypeScript case) will enforce the type constraints at **development time** and save you tons of trouble at least. This already gives you much more safety.

Let's imagine you have a `Stack`. If you don't make the `Stack` generic you either:

1. Lose type safety and allow for this to happen

```
my_stack.push('foo')
my_stack.push(3)
```

Which will may blow up at runtime if the purpose of this stack instance was to hold only strings.

or

2. You have to create a `StringStack`, `ByteStack`, `IntStack` etc to get type safety at the cost of code duplication. This is essentially what you are doing when you say you'd would like to fix `MemoryDB` to `[byte, byte]`.

But with generics you can make a `Stack<T>` (sorry, using TypeScript syntax for my personal convenience :sweat_smile: :)

and then you can have many different stacks that are - **at development time** - constraint to be type checked.

So the following would be disallowed by mypy/tsc.

```
my_stack = new Stack<string>();
my_stack.push('foo')
// blows up at *development time*
my_stack.push(3)
```

Still you can use your `Stack<T>` to make another *instance* of the stack that is strongly typed to only serve ints.

```
my_stack = new Stack<int>();
my_stack.push(3)
```

That's the beauty of generics which are possible to enforce at *development time* with Python 3.6 type hints.

> Perhaps we need to do a docstring audit?

Yes, at some point we definitely need that. We also need to unify the parameter type conventions used throughout the docs. The downside is that this will touch a lot of code, so this should probably be done at a time where there are no open PRs.

An `exit` here is putting the responsibility in the wrong place. The plugin system should run each plugin-load in a `try`/`except` statement, catch any thrown exceptions and either exit (if the plugin is essential) or continue (if the plugin can just be disabled). It is not up to the plugin to decide that the whole of Ganga must shutdown because it failed to load.

To put it another way, there's a difference between library and application code. Library code (e.g. the LHCb plugin) should never call things like `exit` because you never know how the application (the Ganga runtime) is using it.

This isn't how the plugin directory was intended to be used. It looks like you're expecting all plugin data for every run to be located in a single plugins folder, which is separate from the runs. As in the following example:

```
root_logdir/
  plugins/
    profile/
      run1/
      run2/
  run1/
    ...
  run2/
   ....
```

However, this is not robust; if, for example, you point tensorboard just at an individual run (logdir=root_logdir/run1) then we lose access to the plugin data. Instead, the following directory structure is intended:

```
root_logdir/
  run1/
    plugins/
      profile/
      trace
  run2/
    plugins/
      profile/
        trace
```
This way, all run data is always packaged within that run's folder.

For implementation, you would need to iterate over every subdirectory of the root directory, and test whether it has the plugin_logdir you're interested in. 

Fortunately, the EventMultiplexer is able to do this all for you. See: [PluginAssets](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/master/tensorboard/backend/event_processing/event_multiplexer.py#L202) on the multiplexer. It handles the run multiplexing itself.

When the plasma store evicts an object, it will evict a bunch at a time (e.g., 20% of the memory). This means that the local scheduler will receive a ton of calls to handle_object_removed all at once, and each one will scan over the full waiting_task_queue and dispatch_queue. It's possible that this could be a performance problem.

Do you think this will be an issue?

In principle, we could keep around the mapping from "object ID" to "tasks that depend on that object ID", and when we get a notification that an object was removed, we can look up those tasks in that mapping, and if they have a bit recording which queue they are in or something like that, then we can immediately move them around as appropriate.

Another solution could be to change this method (later) to take a batch of object IDs and also change the notification channel from the plasma store to the local scheduler to send a batch of notifications.

What do you think about this?
